title: Vasconia 4-Cup Granite Molcajete Mortar and Pestle, Gray
description:
About this item
AUTHETNIC TOOL FOR GRINDING: Enjoy an authentic, hands-on tool for grinding grains, spices, and herbs, as well as making salsas, guacamole, and pico de gallo.
LARGE CAPACITY FOR GRINDING: The beautiful granite Molcajete has a 4-cup capacity, this mortar and pestle looks great on the counter and also looks beautiful on the table serving a hand-prepared salsa.
THE IDEAL GRINDING TOOL: The smooth interior is an ideal grinding surface and overall it bears a classic, traditional design.
EASY TO CLEAN: Do not place in dishwasher, for best results hand wash and set out to dry.
DIMENSIONS: Overall dimension: 3.4 H x 7.9 W x 7.9 D inches, product weight 7.05 pounds.
reviews_5s:
Nice heavy duty set for herbs, avocados, kitchen stuff. It’s easy to clean and it fully functionalOk, I bought this due to reviews and the size. Very happy I did pick this one. The smaller ones are not practical for anything more than grinding spices. This one so far I've made (actual recipe names) Salsa Verde de Molcajete, Guacamole de Molcajete, and another guacamole using avocados and tomatillos. The size is nice as the ingredients don't come out. It holds enough for a group, and is kind of fun to make in front of people. The quality is great, very sturdy. Some key points, if you just got one and don't know, read all of the following. NEVER wash with soap!

Molcajete Cure Recipe Instructions:

1.-Thoroughly clean the Mexican Molcajete with soap and water.
2.-With your pestle, grind some garlic up to a paste and spread all over the inside of the mortar.
3.-Leave it to rest, through a whole night.
4.-The next day rinse away the garlic from the mortar and presto! your on your way to prepare your delicious Mexican salsas and guacamoles.
5.- This Mexican recipe also can be used to prepare(cure) you cast iron plate or comal, where you can warm up your tortillas, quesadillas, sopes and Mexican tamales.

2 MORE RECIPES TO CURE MOLCAJETES

1) Baking the Molcajete:

Inside the molcajete itself, create a paste using onion, garlic, and cooking oil as the base. Spread the paste and be sure to cover all the pores on the inner part of the molcajete. Then place it inside the oven and bake it for 30 minutes at 300 degrees so that the paste thoroughly permeates the stone. Once the molcajete has cooled off, wash it thoroughly with soap and water.
2) Boiling the Molcajete:

Place the molcajete on the stove grill and fill it with water and a lot of grain salt and vinegar. Allow the water to boil and let it be thoroughly consumed. Now let it cool off and cover any left over pores with uncooked rice, using the tejolote (pestle) to grind.

Finally, as a test:

Before using a Molcajete for your first salsa: grind up some sea salt (thick) then some crushed corn until they come out clean and free of debris. Finally, clean the molcajete with a scrub and water and the molcajete is ready for use.

Try one of the two curing methods and do a test. This should take care of the shedding problem and you will be ready to make your salsas.

Alternate info:
You must season your molcajete before you use it. Otherwise whatever you prepare in it will just taste like dirt/rock. Place a handful of rice in the molcajete and grind until it is gray. Throw this rice out and repeat until the ground rice is still white. This may take quite some time. Best to do it over a few hours or a few days, otherwise you will end up with a sore wrist and shaved knuckles. Happy Cooking!

Rock Salt is the key to seasoning a brand new " molcajete ". Using the "pestle" that came with the bowl, put in some rock salt and " grind " it into all surfaces of the molcajete. When the rock salt becomes table salt rinse the bowl. Repeat this procedure until the inside of the bowl is smooth to the touch. This will take at least one-two hours of " work " to make the surface smooth, depending on the size of the molcajete. This may seem tedious, but it is well worth it.

Here is another tip about the preparation and care of your newly acquired molcajete. First, soak the molcajete for 8-12 hours in lukewarm water to fill the porous surface and allow the flavors you will soon add to soak in. Then, take some of your favorite spices (we recommend a few cloves of garlic, a tablespoon of comino (cumin), salt and pepper) and grind vigorously until they are mostly absorbed by the surface.

Lastly, you should not use dish soap to clean your molcajete, nor should you put it in the dishwasher. A thorough rinse with hot (very hot) water shortly after using is all the cleaning your molcajete will ever need. Just like your trusty cast iron skillets, it only gets better with age.Looks nice, its a quality product!I ordered this knowing it wasn’t volcanic rock, but got it because it was granite. Overall it is large, like the description says, and I think it is perfect for grinding of spices and avocado and herbs for whatever you may need, I would recommend looking up how to season most recommendations are to grind a price a few times and then just rinse it out with water. It will see it first that it has a fair amount of gray powder which is just kind of the looser stone but after going through with about two rounds of rice, it should be pretty clean and good to use for food, I can’t wait to use this. I smoke food and use a lot of spices and it will make grinding them up a lot easier. I wish I love making guacamole in and have a little herb garden so I’ll be able to make pesto and other things in it overall it seems very durable. It looks great, but just be aware that it is pretty heavy.I wanted a Molcajete but have never really loved the porous surface of the lava. Decided to try this one and see how it worked. FIrst, it's a solid chunk of granite. Since it's granite, it's much smoother than a traditional lava unit and it cleans easy. Heats up well and holds plenty of salsa/chicken/beef/shrimp for the two of us with a lot left over. Great piece for the price.I grew up watching my grandmother and mother use a molcajete. I used one for years too. I have been without one for about 10 years now and decided that it was time to purchase a new one. I looked at a lot of the options and was initially interested in purchasing the traditional volcanic lava style. I kept seeing the granite style ones and they grabbed my attention from a practical standpoint with it comes to curing the molcajete. I also watched several videos about the granite ones and decided to give this one a try.
I am very happy and pleased with this molcajete in every respect. The bowl surface is smooth compared to the volcanic lava, but it still has plenty of texture and grip, as does the pestle. The lack of pitting in the granite makes for a far easier curing process. I took the time to grind rice into a powder in the bowl, but in the end, it probably would gave been just fine had I skipped the process as the surface is essentially ready to go right out of the box. I would recommend this product to anyone. I know that it will last for years and can easily become a family heirloom. Additionally, the box says it has a limited lifetime warranty. Enjoy!I've been wanting a mortar and pestle for a very long time but I never found one that I really liked enough to buy. This one caught my eye because of the larger size. I've always shied away from the really small ones since I don't work with spices and such and that's all they seemed to be good for crushing. Some reviews complained that the inside was rough, but I like that aspect. The pestle is very comfortable in my hand and my hands are small. The wide top makes it easy to work inside of the mortar. It's very heavy and sturdy and mine looks exactly like the one pictured here.

I'm primarily using mine to grind bones into bone meal right now and it works great. I use the dried bones (chicken and turkey) for making bone meal to use in making treats for my dog. It doesn't take too long, nor is it too hard to crush up the cup of bone meal that my recipe requires as a rough powder. The wide opening helps me to monitor for shards and such that could be a problem.

I'm also planning to try making hummus with it and for breaking up nuts and such into smaller pieces. I was recently treated to a common "condiment" in Guatemala (I think) of pumpkin seeds ground into a rough powder that was quite nice on salads. This mortar and pestle will be perfect for crushing up some of that for my table.

I'm very happy with my purchase and I'm sure this will meet my needs for years to come.Will try to take video of it in use. Rough to grind well. Heavy a should be. Very pleasedFrom the images it looked like I was going to have to polish the inside of the bowl quite a bit. Actually the mortar and pestle were very smooth to begin with. After grinding them together they got smoother still but the grooves left by the tooling were nearly microscopic. It arrived in good order, no cracks no defects. Very pleased.This is an excellent tool for making guacamole, or in my case, pesto! The surface in this bowl is moderately rough - I'd call it halfway between the volcanic material of a true molcajete and a smoother marble or porcelain mortar and pestle.

A couple of points:

1) This product comes PRE-SEASONED. You can cure it a couple times a year to keep it up if you want, but you definitely don't have to spend hours grinding it down unless you're a masochist. I tested this out by grinding some white rice and salt in it; the result was pure white powder, without any gray residue or bits of rock as with an unseasoned molcajete.

2) This product is made in China, it says so on the bottom of the box. It's granite, but there's enough wiggle room in how things are worded that it could be a mix of granite and concrete. Either way, I doubt there's anything toxic in the mixture. It's also well-balanced and sturdy.

I can't quibble about performance. It allowed me to mash pine nuts, garlic, and basil leaves to a perfect paste and create the best pesto I've ever had. There's a big difference in making pesto from fresh basil leaves compared to using a food processor.

Taste-wise, the basil flavor is more intense. Best of all, the fauxcajete pesto is much creamier, likely from the smashing of the pine nuts into pine-butter paste!

Instead of just topping the pasta with shreds of leaves and blobs of pesto like bits of glitter, it turned the linguine completely green like spinach pasta when I tossed it in.

Check out my pictures comparing my two bowls of pesto-and-parm topped linguine, you'll see what I mean!
reviews_1s:
This is NOT a molcajete. It is not made of volcanic rock (or granite). This appears to be nothing more than cast cement with flecks of a rock-like substance mixed in to make it appear more rock-like. This mortar and pestle smells horrible (smell does not go away if you wipe/rinse/clean it) and like weird chemicals. There is a dusty covering all over it, which comes off continuously, even after wiping and rinsing. I do not think this would be safe to prepare or cook food in with that dust coming off of it. It looks a lot like cement dust, which comes off even more if you attempt to grind the mortar and pestle together at all. Cement dust is not safe to inhale/eat. I am returning mine for a safe, real molcajete made of lava rock.I was automatically skeptical when I opened the box because it was REALLY DUSTY, and had a smell to it. my aunt told me she wasn’t very convinced with it and compared it to her authentic, made in Mexico, molcajete. I decided to give it a chance and started curing it, I even tried it out with some kosher salt and rice, boiled water, tried everything to make the odor go away, the odor went away, however when it dried out I noticed a lot of shiny spots and paid closer to attention to it and it looked like the more you scratch it/ grind on it, the more these shiny spots are exposed, so beware of this. This is definitely not stone, the description needs to be updated, because it is also not granite, it’s just the top coat on it that makes it look as if it’s made of 100% granite.Play Video
The molcajete is very nice, the pestle came used as it is smoothed out, but there a pieces of salsa and old guac stuck to it…which is gross. It’s weird because the molcajete itself is brand new but the pestle was previously used. The good news is with a thorough wash itl be fine and it’s already cured by the previous owner so I won’t have to cure it.

I bought another molcajete from a different vendor and all components came brand new with that one so I have made a comparison video and pictures. Just know if you order this, there is a chance it will come used with food on it.I can’t give a review on the quality or usability of this product, because we never made it that far. When my wife opened it (for her Mother’s Day gift) she thought one of our children passed gas. After we were satisfied that it was t one of them, we found that it was the bowl! I don’t usually write reviews, but this time it’s different. No exaggeration, is smelled like this bowl had been used to store poop. Obviously even if we could get the smell out, I could not bring myself to eat guacamole that was made in it, so it is now back in the box and ready to be returned.Well if I needed something to breaking big rocks into little rocks in my kitchen, this would be the perfect mortar and pestle. Don't let the picture fool you, the bowl interior is so roughly cut that you can't scrape out your preparation without leaving food behind in the cracks and crevices of the surface and eventually washing this down the kitchen sink. There's no point in measuring any ingredients into this bowl because you'll never know how much the bowl itself will take out of your measurements. The pestle is too short and your hand will end up in your preparation, which is a really bad idea with hot chilies. On the other hand, the feet on the bowl are nicely done and if you need a nice base for large scented candles (if that's your thing) this bowl would be great. At $29.99 this was not a good purchase or value. For $17.00 the 2+ cup granite mortar and pestle from ImportFoods is superior.I purchased this product hoping to have a beautiful piece to make my salsas and guacamole in. I have done the curing process over a 4 day period. This is not getting smooth as it should. I am finding that dust particles are still coming off in my food. I am not pleased with this product at all.I'm returning it. A chalky residue comes off every time I gently run my hands over it! I don't want that in my food!!I live in an area that I have granite near my house. If you take a knife and try to cut granite it will show no sign of wear. If you do the same with this product you can easily cut a groove in it with a butter knife. I am not a geologist but this molcajete is likely something other than granite. Not sure how you find the real deal. Almost all of these cheap molcajetes are made in China. The fact that I don't know what its made of concerns me about its safety.I disliked that it arrived broken before I had a chance to use it. In the process of requesting a replacement or refund.After washing and curing still very dusty. Definitely not granite, worried about the dust in my food. My other granite molcajetes are a much stronger material.
